metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `verify_attestation_record` sources the HMAC secret for legacy dispatch — e.g., does it accept an optional `key` parameter, derive it from `repo_root` via an existing helper, or read it from the tracker — so the wrapper signature is unambiguous and AC #7's read-both test covers the HMAC branch end-to-end.
findings.finding: The read-path wrapper `verify_attestation_record` is shown with the illustrative signature `(record, ticket_id, *, repo_root)` — it carries NO `key` parameter — but all three existing callers it replaces (`verify_signature`, `verify_attestations`, `signature_findings`) currently pass a raw HMAC secret to `verify_record(record, ticket_id, key)`. For the expand-phase legacy dispatch branch to work, the wrapper must somehow source the HMAC secret, yet the plan never specifies where it comes from (derived from `repo_root`? read from the tracker? passed as an additional parameter?). An implementer following the plan literally will either break legacy HMAC verification or be forced to make an undocumented design choice.
findings.location: What section — 'Read path (expand)' / wrapper dispatch description; also AC #7 (expand-phase read-both) and AC #11 (dispatch routing test)
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'Read path' section: 'The wrapper accepts an additional keyword argument `hmac_key: str | None = None`; for legacy `signature`-bearing records it passes `hmac_key` directly to the unchanged `verify_record(record, ticket_id, key)`; callers that previously passed the key explicitly now pass it as `hmac_key=` to the wrapper.' Update AC #11 to assert the legacy HMAC branch uses the key parameter.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and specify the exact `ssh-keygen -y -f <private_key_path>` invocation (with the mandatory `-f` flag) for `.opcert-key.pub` re-derivation, and add a test that exercises pub-key recovery in a headless (no-tty) environment.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'ssh-keygen -y' as the mechanism for re-deriving .opcert-key.pub from the committed private key, but does not specify the mandatory '-f <private_key_path>' flag. Without '-f', ssh-keygen -y prompts interactively for a filename, which will hang a subprocess call indefinitely in the signing path — a silent deadlock in production.
findings.location: What section — Environment key genesis: '.opcert-key.pub is DERIVATIVE, never a commit point — written from the committed private key (ssh-keygen -y) and re-derivable any time it is missing.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the bare 'ssh-keygen -y' reference with 'ssh-keygen -y -f <tracker>/.opcert-key' in the plan's genesis description, and add a note that the subprocess must be called with capture_output=True and no stdin to avoid an interactive hang.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the full `ssh-keygen -y -f <tracker>/.opcert-key` invocation (with mandatory `-f` flag) for `.opcert-key.pub` re-derivation to prevent a subprocess hang on missing pub-key recovery.
findings.finding: The plan specifies pub-key re-derivation as 'ssh-keygen -y' (body, Environment key genesis section) without the mandatory '-f <private_key_path>' flag. Invoked as a bare `ssh-keygen -y` subprocess, OpenSSH prompts interactively for the key file path, hanging the process indefinitely instead of printing the public key to stdout.
findings.location: What > Environment key genesis: '.opcert-key.pub is DERIVATIVE, never a commit point — written from the committed private key (ssh-keygen -y) and re-derivable any time it is missing.'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'written from the committed private key (`ssh-keygen -y`)' to 'written from the committed private key (`ssh-keygen -y -f <tracker>/.opcert-key`)' and add a note that the subprocess must be called with `capture_output=True` and no tty.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the read-path section how verify_attestations integrates with the new wrapper: e.g. whether it calls _resolve_and_reduce once and passes the resolved key+pub-key to the wrapper (via an internal helper), or whether the wrapper is given an optional resolved-state parameter to avoid repeated I/O.
findings.finding: The plan says verify_attestations (signing.py:604-618) moves to the new shape-aware wrapper, but verify_attestations currently receives a raw HMAC key from _resolve_and_reduce() and passes it directly to verify_record(); the new wrapper (verify_attestation_record(record, ticket_id, *, repo_root)) carries no key parameter — the plan never specifies how verify_attestations resolves its key for the wrapper call, leaving the refactoring protocol underspecified.
findings.location: What section — 'Read path (expand)' subsection; src/rebar/signing.py:604-618

findings.finding: The plan specifies the `verify_attestation_record` wrapper routes envelope records to `attest.registry.verify(OPCERT_KIND, envelope, trust_root)`, which returns a `registry.Verdict(verified, verdict, reason)` dataclass. But no mapping from `Verdict` to the existing `verify_signature_result` dict shape is specified — the dict shape requires `manifest`, `step_count`, `algorithm`, `key_id`, `signed_at`, `head_sha`, `verified_at_sha`, `rebar_version` fields (all populated by `verify_record`'s `base` dict). The plan also asserts a foreign-principal op-cert surfaces a `"foreign_key"` verdict, but `SshsigScheme.verify()` produces `"mismatch"` (not `"foreign_key"`) when the allowed_signers don't match (proven by `sshsig.py:116`). The translation rule from `Verdict` → result dict is unspecified.
findings.location: What section — 'Read path (expand)'; `src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py` line 116; `src/rebar/signing.py` lines 372–387

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out note describing what happens if this story must be reverted post-landing (e.g., 'revert causes envelope attestations to read as unsigned; re-running the affected gate re-earns a fresh attestation under the restored HMAC path — same lifecycle as stale attestations') so operators have a recovery path.
findings.finding: Once envelope-bearing attestations are written to the ticket store, a full code revert (back to the pre-expand version) would leave those records unverifiable by the legacy HMAC verify path — the plan has no explicit back-out plan for this data-mutation scenario.
findings.location: What section (global seam repoint, read-both), Scope, Acceptance Criteria generally
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection to Scope: 'If this story is reverted after op-cert attestations have been written, those attestations will read as unsigned on the reverted HMAC-only verify path. Recovery: re-run the affected gate on each impacted ticket; HMAC attestations are re-earned immediately. No data migration is required because the attestations map is last-writer-wins and the old kind key is reused.'

findings.finding: The plan does not assert the schema/behavioral state after rollback to pre-story code when envelope-bearing records already exist in the store. Old code's `verify_record` expects a `signature` (HMAC hex) field; envelope records written by new code omit it (`signature` becomes `NotRequired`). Rolling back would cause old code to treat all newly-certified envelope records as unsigned, re-blocking gates on already-certified tickets — and `signing.py:648`'s `record['signature'][:16]` would `KeyError` on those records.
findings.location: Scope section / AC line 'sign_result.schema.json and SignResultOut admit the envelope-bearing result shape' / 'Degrade, never wedge' section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback posture' sub-section under Scope: (1) old code's `verify_record` is updated in this story (or a thin shim is backported) to return `{certified: false, verdict: 'unverified', reason: 'envelope-record-unsupported'}` rather than KeyError when `signature` is absent; (2) a compensating-forward note states that if rollback occurs, affected tickets require a gate re-run to re-earn their attestation. This keeps the post-rollback state well-defined and non-crashing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback section (or AC) that explicitly asserts the schema/data state after rolling back the expand story: either (a) document that rollback is 'compensate-forward only' (re-run affected gates on new code after hotfix), naming the exact recovery operator action, or (b) provide a migration script that converts any envelope records back to HMAC before reverting, with a literal `DATA LOSS RISK: none` / `DATA LOSS RISK: re-certification required` label.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the state of persisted envelope-bearing attestation records after a rollback of this story. Old code (pre-story) checks for the `signature` field and, per the plan's own description of `signature_findings()`, would report envelope-bearing records as `unsigned`. A rollback therefore silently degrades already-certified tickets to `unsigned`, potentially blocking claim/close gates with no recovery path described.
findings.location: 'Rollback / Data-Loss' — absent from plan; related language in 'Downstream `signature`-field consumers' and 'Scope' sections; AC line 'make lint && make typecheck && make test green'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback note' sub-section under Scope: 'Rolling back this story after any envelope records are written requires re-running the affected gate producers on the new code (compensate-forward); reverting to pre-story code without clearing envelope records will cause those attestations to read as `unsigned` on old code. No data is destroyed — envelope records remain in the store — but re-certification is required. Document this as a known operational consequence in the runbook.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that 'signature' is removed from sign_result.schema.json's 'required' array (making it an optional property) as part of the three-artifact co-update, so the schema accepts envelope-bearing records in the expand phase.
findings.finding: The plan says sign_result.schema.json 'admits BOTH result shapes' in the expand phase but never specifies removing 'signature' from the schema's 'required' array — leaving it as a hard JSON Schema requirement that rejects the envelope-bearing result shape.
findings.location: What section — 'Output-schema contract' subsection; src/rebar/schemas/sign_result.schema.json:8
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section's schema-update description, add: 'In sign_result.schema.json, remove "signature" from the required array; it becomes an optional property alongside the new optional "envelope" and "principal" fields.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the `verify_attestation_record` wrapper to a specific module (e.g., `src/rebar/signing.py` alongside `verify_record`) and remove the 'e.g.' hedge on the function name in the 'Read path (expand)' section.
findings.finding: The `verify_attestation_record` wrapper function name and module location are introduced with 'e.g.' hedging, leaving the executor to decide its home, which affects the import paths for all three EVERY-caller sites enumerated in the plan.
findings.location: Body — 'Read path (expand)' section; phrase: 'a shape-aware wrapper (e.g. `verify_attestation_record(record, ticket_id, *, repo_root)`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a shape-aware wrapper (e.g. `verify_attestation_record(...)`)' with 'a shape-aware wrapper `verify_attestation_record(record, ticket_id, *, repo_root)` added to `src/rebar/signing.py` directly below `verify_record`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the expected permission mode for `.opcert-key.pub` in both the 'Environment key genesis' body section and AC2 (suggest 0644 as the standard public-key convention).
findings.finding: The public key file `.opcert-key.pub` mode is never specified; the body and AC2 state 0600 for the private key but are silent on the pub key permission, leaving the executor to guess.
findings.location: Body — 'Environment key genesis' section; AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the genesis paragraph: 'the public key is written mode 0644 (world-readable, consistent with OpenSSH conventions)' and add to AC2: '…pub key mode 0644…'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit no-mock constraint to at least one of AC3/AC4/AC5/AC6 — e.g., 'the test must use the real `sign_opcert_manifest` and `opcert_from_record` from the e4df stack (no patching of those symbols)' — so that CI green for these ACs proves the live integration path, not a stub proxy.
findings.finding: ACs 3, 4, 5, and 6 each say 'a test asserts [envelope-bearing result]' but none of them carry a no-mock constraint; under rubric E6(f), the global seam repoint is a cutover to a new default path backed by the e4df external dependency, so at least one AC must explicitly require the real `sign_opcert_manifest`/`opcert_from_record` machinery rather than a patch/stub — otherwise green CI can satisfy every AC without the new live path ever executing.
findings.location: AC3, AC4, AC5, AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC3: '(the test must not patch `sign_opcert_manifest` or `opcert_from_record`; the e4df round-trip established in AC1 must remain live throughout the test suite).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that explicitly validates `verify_signature_result.schema.json` contains the four new verdict strings (`invalid`, `unavailable`, `unknown_kind`, `unknown_scheme`) and that the schema-drift test is regenerated and green.
findings.finding: The `verify_signature_result.schema.json` update — widening the closed `verdict` enum with exactly four new strings (`invalid`, `unavailable`, `unknown_kind`, `unknown_scheme`) and regenerating the schema-drift test — is a named deliverable in the What section with no covering AC.
findings.location: What section: 'verify_signature_result.schema.json's CLOSED verdict enum … is widened with EXACTLY the four strings … with the schema-drift test regenerated'; Acceptance Criteria (all 13 ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] `verify_signature_result.schema.json` is updated to include the four new verdict strings (`invalid`, `unavailable`, `unknown_kind`, `unknown_scheme`); the schema-drift test is regenerated and green; a test validates a real op-cert verify result against the updated schema.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC 11 (or add a dedicated AC) to assert that an envelope-bearing record whose `principal` differs from the local `env_id` is returned as `{verdict: "foreign_key", certified: false}` without invoking the op-cert verifier.
findings.finding: The `foreign_key` dispatch path for envelope records — where `record.principal != own env_id` causes the wrapper to return `{verdict: "foreign_key", certified: false}` without invoking the scheme — is a specified behavioral contract in the What section but is not tested by any AC.
findings.location: What section: 'record.principal != own env_id → the WRAPPER returns verdict: "foreign_key", certified: false WITHOUT invoking the scheme'; AC 11 (read-path dispatch)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC 6 to also assert that the close-gate producer withholds signing for non-PASS verdicts, degraded-coverage tickets, and `source=="local"` tickets, matching the full guard list in the What section.
findings.finding: The What section lists four close-gate never-sign guards that 'are all preserved unchanged' (non-PASS, degraded coverage, material-drift recheck, `certifiable: false` / `source=="local"`), but AC 6 only tests material-drift recheck and `certifiable: false`, leaving non-PASS, degraded-coverage, and `source=="local"` withhold rules without coverage in the close-gate AC.
findings.location: What section: 'Close-gate producer: … the never-sign structural guards (non-PASS, degraded coverage), the material-drift recheck, and the certifiable: false / source=="local" withhold rules are all preserved unchanged'; AC 6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to Scope: (a) an ADR or CLAUDE.md entry capturing the 'fork A — uniform producer signing' architectural decision and its rationale, and (b) a README or runbook section covering the `.opcert-key` key-management lifecycle (genesis, loss/regeneration, git-ignore) and the `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` deployment config knob.
findings.finding: The plan introduces significant operator-facing artifacts and an architectural decision with no corresponding documentation update scoped: (1) The 'fork A — uniform producer signing' decision (settling the epic's Producer-signing & compute-runtime design resolution) is a major architectural choice that is described but no ADR or CLAUDE.md entry is listed in Scope or ACs. (2) The new key-management pattern — `.opcert-key`/`.opcert-key.pub` genesis, loss/regeneration semantics, git-ignore requirement, and the `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` override — is operator-facing config with no README, runbook, or CLAUDE.md update mentioned anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: ## Scope / ## What (key genesis + REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID sections) / ## Why (fork A decision)
findings.suggested_fix: Add one ADR (e.g., `docs/adr/NNN-uniform-producer-signing.md`) covering the fork-A decision, the DSSE op-cert substrate choice, and the key-genesis/loss model. Add a CLAUDE.md or README section titled 'Op-cert key management' listing the key file locations, permissions, git-ignore requirement, regeneration semantics, and the REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID override — mirroring how the existing .signing-key pattern is (or should be) documented.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a subprocess timeout (e.g. 10 s) to both `ssh-keygen` calls (genesis and `ssh-keygen -y` re-derivation) and map a timeout expiry to the existing degrade path (`{signed: false, error: 'ssh-keygen timed out'}`) so a hung subprocess cannot block gate operations.
findings.finding: No timeout or circuit-breaker is specified for the `ssh-keygen` subprocess calls (key genesis via `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N '' -f <staging>/key` and pub-key re-derivation via `ssh-keygen -y`). On entropy-starved or misconfigured systems `ssh-keygen` can block indefinitely, stalling the signing operation — and, because signing is in the gate critical path, stalling the entire gate run — with no bound.
findings.location: What — Environment key genesis section; AC: 'With ssh-keygen unavailable … a plan-review PASS records the in-band {signed: false, …} outcome'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap both `subprocess.run`/`Popen` calls with `timeout=10` (or configurable via `REBAR_OPCERT_KEYGEN_TIMEOUT`); catch `subprocess.TimeoutExpired` and route it to the same `{signed: false, error: …}` in-band outcome as `ensure_available()` failure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add structured log emissions (at WARNING or above) for: key genesis (success with path, failure with reason), degraded-mode activation (ssh-keygen unavailable / key unreadable+unregenerable), verify dispatch branch taken (envelope vs. HMAC), and foreign-key classification — so operators can observe and alert on op-cert health without inspecting individual attestation records.
findings.finding: No observability instrumentation (structured log line, metric, or trace event) is specified for new failure points introduced by the op-cert seam: key genesis success/failure, degraded-mode activation (ssh-keygen unavailable or key unreadable), verify-dispatch routing (envelope vs. HMAC branch taken), or foreign-key classification. Operators cannot distinguish a correctly-functioning op-cert system from one silently degraded to unsigned attestations.
findings.location: What — 'Degrade, never wedge' section; Read path section; Key genesis section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an env-var flag (e.g. `REBAR_OPCERT_WRITE=1`) that gates the global `sign_manifest` repoint so operators can disable op-cert writing and fall back to HMAC without a code deploy, OR explicitly document why a code-rollback is acceptable (e.g. single-binary CLI with no long-running server state in this story's scope) and confirm the rollback procedure is tested.
findings.finding: The global repoint of `sign_manifest` is a 100%-traffic flip with no in-process feature flag. If op-cert signing malfunctions in production (e.g., key genesis silently broken, DSSE envelope rejected by a downstream verifier not yet updated), reverting requires a full code rollback deploy — there is no runtime switch to fall back to HMAC signing for the write path.
findings.location: What — 'The seam is `signing.sign_manifest` itself — a global repoint'; Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add failure-path tests for `verify_attestation_record`: assert that a record with a corrupted/truncated envelope returns `verdict: 'invalid'` (not an exception), a record with neither `signature` nor `envelope` returns a defined non-certified verdict, and (if the dispatch rule resolves it) a record with both fields routes deterministically.
findings.finding: AC11's read-path dispatch test covers only the happy-path routing (envelope → op-cert verifier, signature → HMAC path) but specifies no test for malformed or ambiguous record shapes — e.g. a record claiming `algorithm: 'sshsig'` with a truncated/corrupted envelope, a record carrying neither a `signature` nor an `envelope` field, or a record with both fields. The `verify_attestation_record` wrapper is a new user-facing code path (its output reaches `verify_signature()` and MCP consumers); without a failure-path test, a corrupted-envelope record that raises instead of returning `verdict: 'invalid'` would be undetected.
findings.location: AC: 'Read-path dispatch' (verify_attestation_record, AC11); What section 'Read path (expand)'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC11 to include: '…and a record with a malformed envelope payload returns `verdict: "invalid"` without raising; a record with neither field returns a defined non-certified verdict; ambiguous both-field shape is handled deterministically per the dispatch rule.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add tests that drive `verify_attestation_record` with an envelope whose kind is not registered (`unknown_kind`) and with a correctly-shaped envelope whose scheme entry is absent (`unknown_scheme`), asserting the exact verdict string propagates through `verify_signature()` unchanged and matches the schema enum values.
findings.finding: The four new verdict strings introduced by the op-cert verify path — `'unknown_kind'` and `'unknown_scheme'` (from `registry.verify`) — have no AC that exercises the code path producing them. Every AC that touches the verify path uses a correctly registered kind/scheme, so these registry-defensive verdicts are added to `verify_signature_result.schema.json` (widening the enum) but are never tested to actually flow through `verify_attestation_record` → `verify_signature()` to a caller. If the mapping logic drops or renames them, the schema and code diverge silently.
findings.location: What section 'Read path (expand)' — verdict string table; AC10 (schema enum widening); AC11
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC11: '…and that a record verified against an unregistered kind returns `verdict: "unknown_kind"`, and against an unregistered scheme returns `verdict: "unknown_scheme"`, with these strings matching the schema enum values.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` is distinct from the existing `REBAR_ENV_ID` / `_seam.env_id()` identity surface, and if so document the specific deployment scenario that requires a *separate* signing-principal override rather than reusing the existing env-id infrastructure; also note that `docs/env-vars.md` must be regenerated (`python scripts/gen_env_registry.py`) for the CI drift gate to pass.
findings.finding: `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID` is a new env-var config key that may duplicate the existing per-environment identity surface (`_seam.env_id(tracker)` reading `.env-id`, and the existing `REBAR_ENV_ID` used in the reconciler). The plan says the principal defaults to 'the environment's existing env_id', which is already provided by `_seam.env_id()` / `.env-id`. Adding a third identity-override var without explaining why the existing `REBAR_ENV_ID` or `.env-id` cannot serve as the override proliferates the config surface.
findings.location: What section — 'Environment key genesis': 'REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID overrides (deployment config)'; AC8: 'REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID overrides the DSSE principal; absent, the principal equals the environment's env_id'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `opcert.verify_opcert` (the claimed emitter of `key_not_valid_at_era` in the op-cert era path) exists in the e4df stack and is confirmed by the dependency-gate AC import check; add it to the AC's `python -c` probe if so.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'opcert.verify_opcert' exists and emits 'key_not_valid_at_era' (cited as the merge-gate era path), but `verify_opcert` has zero grep hits anywhere in the repository including the attest/ package.
findings.location: What section — Read path dispatch, verdict string table: '("key_not_valid_at_era" is emitted only by the merge-gate era path, opcert.verify_opcert, and is already in the schema enum.)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `sshsig.py:110/125` citation: line ~125 emits `'invalid'` (not `'unavailable'`); update the 'verbatim from the code' string table to cite the correct line numbers for each verdict string.
findings.finding: The plan cites `sshsig.py:125` as the second location that emits the `'unavailable'` verdict string, but the actual code at line 125 emits `'invalid'` (for an empty/control-char principal), not `'unavailable'`.
findings.location: What section — Read path dispatch, verdict string table: 'The strings this path can emit, verbatim from the code: ... "unavailable" (ssh-keygen missing — sshsig.py:110/125)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change `"unavailable" (ssh-keygen missing — sshsig.py:110/125)` to `"unavailable" (ssh-keygen missing — sshsig.py:110); "invalid" (malformed principal/namespace — sshsig.py:122/127)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the line-number citations for `signing.verify_signature` (actual ~583) and `signing.verify_attestations` (actual ~604) to match the current file; remove or correct the 'grep-verified' claim if these numbers were not freshly verified.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `signing.verify_signature` is at `signing.py:675` and `signing.verify_attestations` is at `signing.py:692`, but the actual locations are ~583 and ~604 respectively — the stated line numbers are off by ~90 lines.
findings.location: What section — Read path: 'EVERY verify_record caller moves to the wrapper — the complete inventory (grep-verified): signing.verify_signature (signing.py:675), signing.verify_attestations (signing.py:692 — the all-kinds iterator)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `signing.py:675` with `signing.py:583` and `signing.py:692` with `signing.py:604`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `.opcert-key` and `.opcert-key.pub` to the `_GITIGNORE` constant in `src/rebar/_commands/init.py` (and enumerate that file as an edit target in the plan's Scope/What sections).
findings.finding: The `_GITIGNORE` constant in `src/rebar/_commands/init.py` (lines 40-52) lists `.signing-key` but not `.opcert-key` or `.opcert-key.pub`. The plan describes the new key as 'git-ignored' but does not enumerate `init.py` as an edit target for adding the new filenames to `_GITIGNORE`.
findings.location: What / Environment key genesis section; src/rebar/_commands/init.py lines 40-52
findings.suggested_fix: Add `.opcert-key\n.opcert-key.pub` lines to the `_GITIGNORE` f-string in `init.py` alongside `.signing-key`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the four new verdict strings (`"invalid"`, `"unavailable"`, `"unknown_kind"`, `"unknown_scheme"`) to the `verdict: Literal[...]` in `VerifySignatureResult` at `src/rebar/types.py:237`, and enumerate `types.py::VerifySignatureResult` as an edit target alongside `types.py::SignResult`.
findings.finding: The `VerifySignatureResult` TypedDict in `src/rebar/types.py:237` has `verdict: Literal["unsigned", "foreign_key", "certified", "mismatch", "key_not_valid_at_era"]`. The plan widens the `verify_signature_result.schema.json` enum and notes the widening, but does NOT mention updating the `types.py::VerifySignatureResult` Literal to include the four new verdict strings (`"invalid"`, `"unavailable"`, `"unknown_kind"`, `"unknown_scheme"`).
findings.location: What / Output-schema contract section; src/rebar/types.py:237

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_outputs.py:239` (`test_sign_and_verify_signature_shapes`) as an existing test that must be updated when `sign_result.schema.json` removes the `signature` required constraint, and note it explicitly in the plan's scope/test section.
findings.finding: The existing integration test `test_sign_and_verify_signature_shapes` in `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_outputs.py:239` validates `rebar.sign_manifest()` output against the current `sign_result.schema.json`, which requires `signature` as a mandatory field. After the seam repoint, `sign_manifest()` will return an envelope-bearing record without `signature`. This existing test is not mentioned in the plan as requiring update, even though it will fail.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC#10 / tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_outputs.py:239-263

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `dir=str(tracker)` (or equivalent same-filesystem parent) for the `mkdtemp` staging directory in the Ed25519 key genesis, mirroring the existing HMAC genesis's `mkstemp(dir=str(key_file.parent))` guard against cross-device `os.link` failure.
findings.finding: The Ed25519 key genesis uses `mkdtemp` without specifying `dir=tracker`, so `os.link` of the private key from the staging dir to `<tracker>/.opcert-key` will raise `OSError(EXDEV)` — not `FileExistsError` — on systems where the OS temp dir and the tracker dir are on different filesystems (e.g. `/tmp` on tmpfs, tracker on ext4). The existing HMAC genesis explicitly guards against this by passing `dir=str(key_file.parent)` to `mkstemp`. This is a hidden atomicity assumption that breaks the exclusive-create commit point on cross-device configurations.
findings.location: What section — 'Environment key genesis' paragraph; compare signing.py:138 `_generate_key_file`
findings.suggested_fix: Change the staging dir creation to `tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix='.opcert-key-staging-', dir=str(tracker_path), mode=0o700)` so the staging dir and target are guaranteed on the same filesystem, making `os.link` succeed (or fail only with `FileExistsError` on a race).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that `opcert.register_opcert_policy()` (or its equivalent) is added to `attest/__init__.py`'s import-time registration sequence, alongside `hmac_legacy`, `sshsig`, and `authorship`, so `POLICY['rebar.opcert.v1']` is populated in every process before `verify_attestation_record` is called.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `opcert.register_opcert_policy()` pins `POLICY['rebar.opcert.v1']` to the `sshsig` scheme, but does not specify that this registration is wired into `attest/__init__.py`'s import-time sequence. Currently `attest/__init__.py` calls `hmac_legacy.register_legacy_schemes()`, `sshsig.register_sshsig_scheme()`, and `authorship.register_authorship_policy()` at import time. If `register_opcert_policy()` is not added there, any `registry.verify(OPCERT_KIND, envelope, root)` call in the new `verify_attestation_record` wrapper returns `Verdict(verified=False, verdict='unknown_kind', ...)` — a closed but incorrect failure on every op-cert verify in a fresh process.
findings.location: What section — 'Read path (expand)' paragraph; attest/__init__.py lines 15-19

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge (and test) that after migration the `refreshed-from:` manifest line embeds the full env_id principal string rather than the historical 16-char hex fingerprint, and confirm this format change is intentional and acceptable for audit/display tooling.
findings.finding: The plan's translation table maps `key_id` on envelope records to `record.principal` (the env_id UUID string), but `drift_refresh_candidate` returns `{manifest, deps, key_id}` where `key_id` was previously a 16-character HMAC hex fingerprint. `refresh_attestation` at `attest.py:529` reads `verify_signature(...).get('key_id', '?')` and embeds this as a `refreshed-from:` provenance string in the new manifest. The plan asserts the `key_id`/provenance read 'works as-is', but the format materially changes from a short hex digest to a full UUID principal string — the claim is correct in that it won't crash, but the plan does not acknowledge that the audit semantics of the `refreshed-from:` field silently change for all post-migration drift-refreshed records.
findings.location: What section — 'Read path (expand)' translation table note and 'Downstream `signature`-field consumers' section; attest.py:396 and attest.py:529

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `types.py::VerifySignatureResult.verdict` to the edit-target list alongside `verify_signature_result.schema.json`, widening its `Literal` to include `'invalid'`, `'unavailable'`, `'unknown_kind'`, and `'unknown_scheme'`.
findings.finding: types.py::VerifySignatureResult.verdict is a CLOSED Literal whose vocabulary is never updated in the plan, even though the new wrapper can emit four new strings ('invalid', 'unavailable', 'unknown_kind', 'unknown_scheme') that are absent from it.
findings.location: What section — 'Output-schema contract' subsection; src/rebar/types.py:237
findings.suggested_fix: In types.py line 237, change the Literal to: `Literal['unsigned', 'foreign_key', 'certified', 'mismatch', 'key_not_valid_at_era', 'invalid', 'unavailable', 'unknown_kind', 'unknown_scheme']` and add it to the What section's three-artifact co-update list.

findings.finding: The plan states the `verify_signature_result` enum should be widened with the op-cert dispatch verdicts including `key_not_valid_at_era`. However, `SshsigScheme.verify()` (the scheme that will be used for op-certs) does NOT emit `key_not_valid_at_era` — that string is specific to the authorship-gate era-check path (`_commands/verify_authorship.py:55`). The actual new strings SshsigScheme emits are `unavailable`, `invalid`, `mismatch`, `certified`, and (from the registry) `unknown_kind`, `unknown_scheme`.
findings.location: What section: 'verify_signature_result.schema.json's CLOSED verdict enum is likewise widened with the FULL set of verdict strings the op-cert dispatch target can emit — taken authoritatively from attest.registry.verify's emitted set (certified / key_not_valid_at_era / mismatch plus the registry's degraded/scheme-unavailable strings)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'certified / key_not_valid_at_era / mismatch' with 'certified / mismatch / unavailable / invalid / unknown_kind / unknown_scheme' as the full op-cert dispatch verdict set.

findings.finding: No acceptance criterion explicitly asserts that the widened `verify_signature_result.schema.json` verdict enum is COMPLETE (i.e. every string `attest.registry.verify` can emit is present in the enum). AC #10 validates a real sign result against the schema and AC #13 requires green tests, but neither catches a schema that is missing a subset of valid verdict strings — the schema-drift test regeneration is mentioned only in the body, not in an AC.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #10; body section 'verify_signature_result.schema.json's CLOSED verdict enum is likewise widened'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the RETIRE/rotation lifecycle for `.opcert-key`: when/how an operator intentionally rotates the key, what happens to existing certified attestations (bulk re-certification vs. per-ticket re-earn), and what the decommission procedure is when a tracker environment is retired — even if the answer for this story is 'manual re-trigger; rotation tooling is out of scope for the expand phase (tracked as follow-on).'
findings.finding: The `.opcert-key` / `.opcert-key.pub` shared state has no RETIRE or rotation lifecycle. The plan describes key-loss as self-healing (a fresh keypair is generated), but there is no rotation schedule, expiry policy, or operator-initiated rotation ceremony. After a key-loss regeneration, all previously certified attestations permanently read as not-certified with no systematic re-certification path beyond 'wait for the next gate run on each ticket'.
findings.location: What — 'Key-loss pre-mortem' section; Environment key genesis section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm with the e4df stack author that `OPCERT_KIND`, `OPCERT_NAMESPACE`, and `register_opcert_policy()` will be importable from `rebar.attest.opcert` (a discrete submodule), or update the plan's import paths to match the actual e4df module layout before implementation begins.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `attest/opcert.py` (providing `OPCERT_KIND`, `OPCERT_NAMESPACE`, and `opcert.register_opcert_policy()`) will exist as a discrete submodule with those named constants, but the installed `rebar.attest.opcert` symbol resolves to `rebar/attest/__init__.py` rather than a dedicated `opcert.py` file, and no `opcert.py` exists in `src/rebar/attest/`.
findings.location: What section — Read path: 'the substrate's OWN kind (the Policy(SshsigScheme, namespace=OPCERT_NAMESPACE) entry opcert.register_opcert_policy() registers; OPCERT_KIND = OPCERT_NAMESPACE = "rebar.opcert.v1", the constants in attest/opcert.py'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'a cert whose principal is another environment surfaces the existing foreign_key-style non-verified outcome,' but no dispatch-wrapper translation rule is specified to produce that outcome. SshsigScheme.verify() (sshsig.py:160-170) returns only 'certified' or 'mismatch' — it never emits 'foreign_key'. The existing foreign_key verdict is produced by signing.py:402-419 (HMAC path, comparing key fingerprints), a code path that the envelope branch bypasses entirely. The plan names no rule in the wrapper that maps a principal-mismatch case to 'foreign_key'.
findings.location: What section — 'Read path (expand)' paragraph: 'a cert whose principal is another environment surfaces the existing foreign_key-style non-verified outcome'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'a cert whose `principal` is another environment surfaces the existing `foreign_key`-style non-verified outcome' from the op-cert verify path, but `SshsigScheme.verify()` returns verdict `'mismatch'` (not `'foreign_key'`) for any ssh-keygen exit-nonzero, including a wrong-principal case. The `foreign_key` distinction is produced today by HMAC key-fingerprint comparison in `verify_record`, not by `SshsigScheme`. The new `verify_attestation_record` wrapper must explicitly map wrong-principal op-cert outcomes to `foreign_key` — but this mapping logic is not specified in the plan.
findings.location: What section, Read path paragraph: 'a cert whose `principal` is another environment surfaces the existing `foreign_key`-style non-verified outcome'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Read path section: before calling `registry.verify`, the wrapper compares the record's `principal` field to the local `env_id`; if they differ, return `foreign_key` immediately (the same short-circuit `verify_record` applies for HMAC key-fingerprint mismatch) rather than calling through to `SshsigScheme.verify()`.

findings.finding: The plan's enum-widening instruction says to add 'certified / key_not_valid_at_era / mismatch plus the registry's degraded/scheme-unavailable strings', but `SshsigScheme.verify()` already emits two additional verdict strings not in this list: `'unavailable'` (ssh-keygen missing, sshsig.py:108-112) and `'invalid'` (malformed principal/namespace, sshsig.py:122-132); `registry.verify()` can also return `'unknown_kind'` and `'unknown_scheme'`. These strings would appear in live verify results for op-cert records once the dispatch is live but would fail the CLOSED `verify_signature_result.schema.json` enum validation, potentially causing MCP `model_validate()` errors or schema-drift test failures.
findings.location: What section — 'Output-schema contract' / 'the FULL set of verdict strings the op-cert dispatch target can emit — taken authoritatively from `attest.registry.verify`'s emitted set'; sshsig.py:108-132

findings.finding: The plan asserts the `verify_signature_result.schema.json` verdict enum will be widened with 'the FULL set of verdict strings the op-cert dispatch target can emit — taken authoritatively from `attest.registry.verify`'s emitted set (`certified` / `key_not_valid_at_era` / `mismatch` plus the registry's degraded/scheme-unavailable strings)'. However, inspecting the actual `SshsigScheme.verify()` implementation in `src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py` shows it emits `'unavailable'` (line 110, ssh-keygen absent), `'mismatch'` (lines 116, 169), `'invalid'` (lines 126, 132 — empty/control-char principal or namespace), and `'certified'` (line 162). The plan's named list includes `key_not_valid_at_era`, which does NOT appear in `SshsigScheme.verify()` at all (it originates from `verify_authorship_at_commit` in `authorship.py`, a different code path), and omits `'invalid'`. The claim of authoritativeness is unverified against the actual emitted set.
findings.location: What section — 'verify_signature_result.schema.json's CLOSED verdict enum is likewise widened'; also AC11 (consumer regressions) and AC10 (schema validation)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the plan's verdict list with the actual SshsigScheme.verify() emitted strings: add 'invalid' and 'unavailable' to the enum; remove 'key_not_valid_at_era' unless a specific code path in the opcert verify chain (distinct from authorship) emits it. The plan's AC10 test should drive a case where the scheme returns 'invalid' (e.g., a crafted envelope with an empty keyid) to ensure schema coverage is real.

findings.finding: The plan's named set of new verdict strings to add to the verify_signature_result schema enum ('certified / key_not_valid_at_era / mismatch plus the registry's degraded/scheme-unavailable strings') omits concrete values that SshsigScheme.verify() and registry.verify() provably emit: 'unavailable' (sshsig.py:111), 'invalid' (sshsig.py:125), 'unknown_kind' (registry.py:100), and 'unknown_scheme' (registry.py:107). The phrase 'taken authoritatively from attest.registry.verify's emitted set' is an instruction to the executor to discover these, not a specification — leaving the schema enum incomplete and the executor to independently grep across the call chain.
findings.location: What section — 'Output-schema contract' paragraph; verify_signature_result.schema.json line 17 (closed enum)
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, replace the parenthetical with the full explicit list: 'widened with `unavailable`, `invalid`, `unknown_kind`, `unknown_scheme`, and `certified` (already present) — taken from sshsig.py:108,122 and registry.py:98,104'. Update `VerifySignatureResult.verdict` Literal in types.py:237 to include all five new strings.

findings.finding: The `verify_signature_result.schema.json` verdict enum is a CLOSED enum (JSON Schema `enum` array); the plan says to widen it only with `certified / key_not_valid_at_era / mismatch`, but `attest.registry.verify()` — the new dispatch target for envelope-bearing records — can also emit `"unknown_kind"` (no policy pinned for the kind) and `"unknown_scheme"` (scheme not registered), as proven by `registry.py:39,100` and `docs/attest-substrate.md:54`. Neither value is listed for addition. A production path where `register_opcert_policy()` has not yet run, or a misconfigured kind, would produce an `"unknown_kind"` verdict that fails schema validation and breaks `VerifySignatureResultOut.model_validate()`.
findings.location: What section — 'Output-schema contract'; `src/rebar/schemas/verify_signature_result.schema.json` line 17; `src/rebar/attest/registry.py` lines 39, 98-102
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Output-schema contract' section, add: 'The wrapper maps `unknown_kind` and `unknown_scheme` registry verdicts to `"mismatch"` (with a reason string) before populating the result dict, so the schema enum stays closed to values callers already handle.'

findings.finding: The plan lists `key_not_valid_at_era` as one of the verdict strings 'the op-cert dispatch target can emit — taken authoritatively from `attest.registry.verify`'s emitted set'. But `key_not_valid_at_era` is NOT emitted by `SshsigScheme.verify()` or `registry.verify()` — it is produced exclusively by `verify_authorship_at_commit()` (the authorship-era layer). The actual op-cert path emits `certified`, `mismatch`, `unavailable`, `invalid`, `unknown_kind`, and `unknown_scheme`. Including `key_not_valid_at_era` and omitting `unavailable`/`invalid`/`unknown_kind`/`unknown_scheme` will produce a closed-enum `verify_signature_result.schema.json` that is both over-inclusive (a string that path never produces) and under-inclusive (misses strings it actually does produce).
findings.location: What section — 'Output-schema contract': '`verify_signature_result.schema.json`'s CLOSED `verdict` enum is likewise widened with the FULL set of verdict strings the op-cert dispatch target can emit — taken authoritatively from `attest.registry.verify`'s emitted set (`certified` / `key_not_valid_at_era` / `mismatch` plus the registry's degraded/scheme-unavailable strings)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'certified / key_not_valid_at_era / mismatch plus the registry's degraded/scheme-unavailable strings' with the exhaustive list from sshsig.py + registry.py: `certified`, `mismatch`, `unavailable`, `invalid`, `unknown_kind`, `unknown_scheme`.

findings.finding: The plan claims 'a cert whose `principal` is another environment surfaces the existing `foreign_key`-style non-verified outcome', but `SshsigScheme.verify()` (sshsig.py:160-170) returns `verdict='mismatch'` — not `foreign_key` — when `ssh-keygen -Y verify` exits non-zero due to an unknown principal. The `foreign_key` verdict only originates from the legacy HMAC path. Unless the shape-aware wrapper explicitly compares the envelope's `principal` field against the local environment's own `env_id`/`key_id` BEFORE dispatching to `registry.verify()`, a foreign-environment op-cert will produce `verdict='mismatch'` — which `_engine_support/validate.py:signature_findings()` (validate.py:118-132) escalates to a MAJOR finding instead of the intended MINOR, misclassifying a legitimate cross-environment cert as potential tampering.
findings.location: What section — 'Read path (expand)' / 'a cert whose `principal` is another environment surfaces the existing `foreign_key`-style non-verified outcome'; validate.py:118-132; sshsig.py:160-170
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the wrapper spec: 'Before calling registry.verify(), read the record's `principal` field. If `principal != local_env_principal` (local_env_principal from `_seam.env_id(tracker)` or `REBAR_OPCERT_ENV_ID`), return the foreign_key verdict directly without invoking ssh-keygen — mirroring verify_record's key-fingerprint early-exit (signing.py:410-419).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
